subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Clear chronological development from motivation through model refinement
Well-labelled sections, tables and figures with sensible numbering
Logical narrative that builds each mathematical section on the last
weaknesses: Minor digressions and occasional repetition of definitions
Abrupt sub-headings (e.g. Bayes section) interrupt flow
Extended quotations and unreferenced code screenshots create redundancy
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration is coherent and logically developed, with good overall structure and relevance (band 3 for Coherence/Development and Organization, band 2 for Conciseness). Minor lapses prevent an exceptional score, so a 3/4 reflects strong but not flawless presentation.

strengths: Generally consistent and correct use of mathematical notation (LaTeX style)
Effective use of tables, plots and multiple curve representations
Logical ordering of arguments from data cleaning through model fitting and testing
weaknesses: Occasional typographical and notation slips (mis-typed summation limits, duplicated symbols)
Some formula blocks lack clarity or required intermediate steps
Variable naming inconsistencies blur derivations at points
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The mathematical communication is good, with mostly clear language, use of multiple representations and logical structure (band 3 across all strands). Minor formatting and notation issues prevent the top mark, so 3/4 is appropriate.

strengths: Personal link to hospital experience grounds the research question
Use of Monte-Carlo simulation and Bayesâ theorem shows independent thinking
Modelling choices (outlier removal, function transformation) reflect a personal approach
weaknesses: Core methods remain largely standard textbook procedures
Creative elements are introductory rather than innovative at every stage
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The submission shows clear evidence of independent thinking, personal approach and creativity (band 2 in each strand), but does not reach the outstanding level. 2/3 reflects good but not exceptional personal engagement.

strengths: Meaningful reflection on model suitability and parameter choices
Thoughtful analysis of SSE vs RMSE and discussion of model limitations
Clear evaluation of outcomes with suggestions for more complex methods
weaknesses: Reflection remains surface-level; deeper probing of assumptions is needed
Limited consideration of synthetic data representativeness and unrealistic values impact
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration provides meaningful reflection and basic critical analysis (band 2 across strands) but lacks the depth and insight for the highest mark. A 2/3 accurately captures this level.

strengths: Relevant mathematics (calculus, regression, probability) directly supports investigation
Mathematics is commensurate with SL/HL level and correctly applied
Mostly accurate calculations with graphs matching derived formulae
weaknesses: Lacks formal subgroup selection criterion and some tool-usage details
Could include significance testing to strengthen statistical claims
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The mathematics used is appropriate, mostly correct and fully at course level (band 4 for relevance, level and understanding). Occasional slips prevent a 5 or 6. Therefore 4/6 is a fair representation of the submissionâs mathematical strength.

strandSelections.reasoning: Arguments are developed step-by-step: data cleaning â coefficient calculation â model fitting â goodness-of-fit testing.  Explanations are mostly clear and logically ordered.  Minor algebraic inaccuracies and typographical clutter occasionally blur the line of reasoning, yet the mathematical story remains understandable and coherent.

Calculations of r, slope/intercept, logistic parameters and error metrics are mostly accurate, and graphs align with derived formulae.  Small algebraic slips (omitted divisors, minor rounding inconsistencies) appear but do not materially affect results, indicating good understanding with only occasional inaccuracy.

The student employs tables (quartiles, SSE comparison), scatter-plots with regression lines, logistic and exponential curves and Monte-Carlo overlays.  These visual and numerical representations complement each other and are referenced in the discussion.  While more careful labelling could improve some figures, the overall use of multiple representations is effective.

The report follows a clear chronological path: motivation, data cleaning, model construction, statistical testing and conclusion.  Each new mathematical section builds on the previous one so the reader can follow how the linear model motivates the logistic model and why further refinements are attempted.  Minor lapsesâe.g. digressions when stating formulae and occasional repetition of background definitionsâprevent the development from feeling âtightâ or exceptionally polished, but overall the narrative remains logical and coherent.

Introducing Monte-Carlo simulation with KDE and applying Bayesâ theorem to update risk show creativity beyond the basic syllabus.  Nonetheless, these ideas are implemented at an introductory level (small sample, binary classification), so the initiative is good but not exceptional.

There is thoughtful analysis comparing SSE and RMSE values, acknowledging how sample size affects SSE and why logistic growth outperforms other models.  The discussion recognises weaknesses (outlier removal, limited variables) but remains largely surface-level; deeper probing into mathematical assumptions would be required for the highest band.

The conclusion evaluates how the logistic model can predict âtypicalâ expenses and quantifies the prediction error.  Limitations of ignoring additional risk factors and outliers are stated, and suggestions for more complex Bayesian methods are mentioned, constituting a clear evaluation though not yet comprehensive with future quantitative testing plans.

The student reflects on model suitability, notes that linear growth is unrealistic after 50 and discusses why setting the carrying capacity at the maximum expense distorts the fit.  Such comments represent meaningful, though not deeply critical, reflection throughout the paper.

Mathematical terminology (covariance, SSE, logistic differential equation, RMSE) and standard notation are generally correct and consistently typeset in LaTeX style.  Occasional slipsâsuch as the mis-typed covariance summation limit and duplicated ânââare present but do not hamper comprehension, keeping communication at a solid but not flawless level.

The studentâs narrative is personal â linking the study to a recent hospital stay â and makes deliberate modelling choices (e.g. transforming the logistic function, limiting Monte-Carlo size due to software constraints).  These elements evidence a personal approach, though not so innovative as to merit the top band.

At 20 pages the exploration contains extended quotations of standard definitions, screenshots of code and large graphics that are not always discussed.  While most content relates to the modelling objective, the explanation of KDE, repeated listing of formulas and lengthy appendix references create noticeable redundancy.  Hence the write-up is mostly relevant but not fully concise.

Correlation analysis, ordinary least squares derivation, analytic solution of the logistic differential equation, exponential modelling, SSE/RMSE calculations and an introductory Bayes application are all relevant to the task.  The mathematics directly supports the investigation and extends beyond simple descriptive statistics.

Choosing a real-life insurance question, sourcing a public dataset, deciding to disregard outliers and devising a Monte-Carlo validation demonstrate clear independent thinking.  However, most methods (IQR outlier test, Pearson r, ordinary least squares) are standard textbook procedures; the work does not reach the âoutstandingâ threshold.

The student supplies an introduction, labelled exploration subsections, a conclusion, bibliography and appendices.  Headings, tables and figures are sensibly numbered and referenced in the text, showing deliberate structural planning.  Some sub-headings (e.g. the Bayes section) appear abruptly and a few long formula blocks interrupt the flow, so the organization is very good rather than exemplary.

The work employs calculus, regression theory and probability at a level fully commensurate with AA HL expectations.  While Monte-Carlo and KDE coding goes slightly beyond the syllabus, the underlying mathematics remains accessible to an HL student, correctly pitched for the level.

annotations.comment: The studentâs personal link to their hospital experience effectively demonstrates personal engagement and motivation for the exploration, grounding the research question in genuine context.
The dataset origin is clearly cited, but the student should reflect on how using a synthetic sample affects representativeness and model validity.
The student identifies three subgroups by eye but does not establish a quantifiable criterion for subgroup selection. Consider formal clustering or a threshold rule.
Table 1âs caption is embedded in the first row cell. Reposition the caption above or below the table to adhere to presentation conventions.
The negative lower limit for medical expenses is unrealistic; discuss how this affects the choice of outlier criteria and data cleaning.
The heading 'Using GDC and Google Sheets' lacks detail. Specify the functions or commands used in each tool for transparency.
The interpretation of the two subgroups would be strengthened by calculating correlation coefficients for each group rather than relying on visual inference alone.
The correlation coefficient formula is correctly stated, but each symbol ($$Cov(x,y)$$, $$sigma_x$$, $$sigma_y$$) should be defined immediately to avoid reader confusion.
Combine the definition of covariance with its citation in separate sentences to maintain logical flow between concept explanation and source attribution.
There is a typographical error in the covariance formula: remove the extra equals sign so that $$Cov(x,y)=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n (x_i-x)(y_i-y)$$ reads correctly.
Define each standard deviation formula in its own display block and label the summation index properly for mathematical clarity.
The correlation coefficient 0.9782 is impressive; include a significance or p-value test to solidify the claim of statistical significance.
When introducing linear regression, briefly state underlying assumptions (e.g., normality, homoscedasticity) to contextualize model appropriateness.
In the formula $$b=y-mx$$ clarify whether $$y$$ and $$x$$ refer to means or specific observations to avoid ambiguous notation.
The introduction of Figure 1 is abrupt. Add a framing sentence explaining how the scatter plot informs the modelling strategy for coherence.
The IQR formula block lacks clear formatting. Separate the lower- and upper-limit expressions into distinct lines and label each term for readability.
The numerical evaluation of slope and intercept includes many decimals. Rounding to two or three significant figures would improve clarity.
Linking the logistic model to real-life healthcare cost patterns demonstrates thoughtful independent application of literature to your data.
The partial-fractions step jumps quickly from the integral to the logs. Include the algebraic decomposition to clarify how coefficients are found.
The integration result is correct, but the narrative refers to a variable P. Ensure consistent notation between derivation and discussion.
The final logistic model derivation is well communicated, showcasing consistent and accurate mathematical notation throughout this section.
Assuming the maximum observed expense as carrying capacity oversimplifies parameter estimation; reflect on alternative fitting methods like least-squares.
The presented solution for C and k is concise, but separate the system of equations from the numeric solution to enhance mathematical clarity.
Your insight that fixing L at the data maximum flattens the curve prematurely is strong; consider re-estimating L via error minimization.
Parameters in the transformed logistic function are stated without derivation. Include a brief note explaining how the new C and L values were chosen.
Acknowledging that the logistic model cannot extrapolate beyond the dataset boundaries shows good critical reflection on model limitations.
